Well, look, don't give them credit, first of all, for like getting it, okay, just because you told it to them, don't mean they got it. And I mean, there's plenty of room for you to fuzzy the edges and do more with the sales people because really what you've married is psychology and technology. Well, the psychology side is available for endless conversation, right? But sort of like in their business, right? So there's more value in the broker who has a bricks and mortar investment than the loan officer who has no investment and can go sell cars tomorrow. And so in your case, there's more ascension value. There's more money to be gotten from, as you say, the entrepreneur, the business owner, the person who's got 10 salespeople working for them than the salesperson themselves. Most they're likely to be there longer. So amongst other things you've got in your base, you have a double-sided coin of movement. Well, so somebody's salesperson, organization A, and quits and goes someplace else. The positive side of that coin is you've got a chance of them introducing you to the new place that they just went. The negative side is if they don't, you probably lost them forever, and salespeople are pretty fluid. They move around a lot, right? So see you in a second. So the business owner guy is probably worth 10, 20, 30, what the sales guy is. And so I would look at, I would do things like I would try and figure out where the 3% came from, what made that happen. Because at this point it happened by accident. So how can we make it happen more on purpose? There may be an issue of having to self-select kind of like I described to him, and let them splinter off and be talked to differently. Right? And you may need to go actually hunt for them with a different message entirely. Right? And that message might be how to make your salespeople a thousand times more productive without having another sales meeting. It might, you know, I don't know what it is, but there's a way to get to that owner. I have a lot of folks too who take my stuff and use it for stuff way off of selling, like their kids, their wives, it's all about building trust and language and they come back with incredible stories. And I'm wondering how to sort of take that stuff and use it without sort of getting away from my core. I would discourage you from it. See, when you move from a category where there's a money payoff to what you do to a category where there's no direct arguable money payoff to what you do, typically what have a lot of bad things happen. Now that's not say there's a lot of, you know, I mean the big broad businesses are all that, but a lot of bad things happen. Money is price and calendar year value of customer tends to go down. Cost of customer acquisition tends to go up. So those businesses tend to be about big numbers, thin margins. Now they may have a bigger equity payoff than our kind of businesses, but you really got to have a big sack of marbles if you're going to play effectively. And if you either don't have or aren't comfortable with a big sack of marbles, you got to think long and hard about playing. Your untapped opportunities where you are are so great. I mean you've got, you're showing people fire. You know, you're showing caveman fire. And that's a very powerful thing to do. So see I think there's, first of all, I think like Bill said, I think there's enormous room for you in the sort of traditional direct marketing industry. And again, probably not, and Weight Watchers was really a bad example. I mean they're so big and dumb, they're a giant pain and he has to deal with, right? But there's a layer beneath them of the, you know, not Omaha Steaks, but the guy that's trying to be Omaha Steaks who's doing Buffalo or he's doing whatever he's doing. And he's got a company that's doing three, four, five million a year and he's got a few in-house people. So he's got a little bit of infrastructure. Your thing can make all the difference in the world to him. And for every Omaha Steaks there's a hundred of them. But you're not going to get to him with the sales path. You're going to have to create another path. But there's enormous opportunity there because he can give you more money. Right? He has reason to give you more money. So thank you, Mike. This is just sort of siphon off people who are going that way. Yes. And just keep that going. Yep. And you can start a second front end. Right, exactly. Okay. Yeah. See, I ran like back to him about the generics. See, so when we were very big in chiropractic and dentistry, I had a generic business and I had a chiropractic dentin business. And largely the never-to-shell-meat, the generic crowd really didn't get that we were over here with this big chirodental business. And most of the chirodental business didn't really get that we were over here. We did it well enough that 20 years later there's still doctors, you know, calling me Dr. Kennedy, which I never wanted that in the first place, but they make the assumption, right, if you're teaching doctors and you're showing up at our conventions and you must be a doctor. So you can be, you can have two completely different identities, you know, and either not have them cross at all, or when they cross it's so late that it's okay. He faces, what he's just described is the same thing. See if he's going to go sell self-defense and confidence to women, the Lieutenant X thing ain't going to cut it, okay? Because like he's, you know, his customer base is limited to like Lee Miltier and Sidney and about, you know, five others, but everybody else is going to run from him screaming. So the Captain Chris deal's there, right? Now if he wants to sell to a bunch of cops and firefighters and ex-military guys, really the Captain Chris thing is all wrong. The Lieutenant X thing was about right, right? They want somebody to walk up and hit him in the head to get their attention and, you know, so, but he can run those two vertical businesses. The only place he's got a problem is when he does mass media, but otherwise he can be running three different businesses with three different identities and eventually if they merge no harm done, right? And he'll be more effective. Now it's not as easy, but it's a lot more effective. I have a specific question for you. What are your thoughts about it? Well, so what you're telling me is it did not produce? No, not in that market. What were you in? The national magazine, Realtor? It's probably in his, I think it's in his collection. It's got a depressed looking sales guy with his head in his hand and the headline is free audio seminar reveals how to eliminate painful sales pitch, cold calling and triple your income, new insider phone secrets, a limit sales rejection on the phone. I'll just read you the opening few lines to get you the entry. If you're like most of the business people that I personally coach at work with, you're probably playing the numbers game faithfully, making your calls each day, but producing very little results. In fact, yada, yada, yada. Now everybody in the room should be able to tell you what I'm about to tell you. Is that specific for real? Of course. So the first problem is the realtor see it and instantly reject it. And if they didn't reject it by the time they got the business people, they reject it there.
